data.title: GSLC: An Underachieving Strategy With No Meaningful Edge Over IVV
data.description:
Today's note is supposed to offer a second look at the Goldman Sachs ActiveBeta® U.S. Large Cap Equity ETF (NYSEARCA:GSLC), a passively managed exchange-traded fund I first covered in December 2022, with a Hold rating.

In my first assessment, I pointed out that GSLC is an "otherwise strong large-cap fund," though with a "mixed" performance. At this point, I should say that nearly three years after the previous analysis, the ETF's performance is not just mixed; it is rather disappointing.

Sure, GSLC does not target the growth factor directly (I will be giving more details on that below), yet its strategy is still mindful of momentum, and even despite that, it has underperformed the iShares Core S&P 500 ETF (IVV) in 2023, 2024, and 2025 YTD. So if nothing changes fundamentally, 2025 will be its 5th year of underperformance in a row.

For clarity, the reason for me benchmarking GSLC's returns against IVV's is that the ETF compares its performance to the S&P 500 index in both the monthly fact card and the prospectus available on its website.

Besides, its inability to outcompete IVV is just a part of the problem. The much more significant issue is that it did not offer sufficient downside protection, as illustrated by its maximum drawdown in 2022, which I will address shortly. It is hard to be bullish on a strategy like that.

Next, as usual, I have scrutinized its factor exposures, only to arrive at a conclusion that it does not offer anything especially different from IVV. So below, I would like to address all these issues in due detail.

Basics Of The Goldman Sachs ActiveBeta® U.S. Large Cap Equity ETF

  • Inception: September 17, 2015
  • The Investment Adviser: Goldman Sachs Asset Management, L.P.
  • AUM: $14.47 billion, as of October 17
  • Expense Ratio: 0.09%
  • 5-Year Annualized Total Return (October 2020–September 2025): 15.18% vs. IVV's 16.45%

What Does The GSLC ETF Do?

As we know from the Goldman Sachs Asset Management website, the core of GSLC's strategy is the Goldman Sachs ActiveBeta® U.S. Large Cap Equity Index.

As explained on page 25 of the prospectus, the construction process has two steps. First, the ActiveBeta® Factor Subindexes are created from the components of the Solactive US Large Cap Index using factor scores. There are four factors in total: value, momentum, quality, and low volatility. For instance, when measuring exposure to the value factor, the following metrics are used:

...the factor score is based on a composite of book value-to-price, sales-to-price and free cash flow-to-price.

Second, the Subindexes are amalgamated, with each receiving an equal weight. The index is "normally rebalanced" quarterly, with quite modest adjustments as illustrated by GSLC's turnover of 17%.

GSLC Performance: Nothing Particularly Convincing

Since its inception, GSLC has underperformed IVV by around 24.5%. It expectedly lagged the other two S&P 500 ETFs as well, namely the Vanguard S&P 500 ETF (VOO) and the SPDR® S&P 500® ETF (SPY). I have not added the SPDR® Portfolio S&P 500® ETF (SPLG) to the comparison, as it switched to tracking the S&P 500 only in 2020.

Next, what about the active return per year with IVV selected as a benchmark? Nothing especially impressive, as GSLC outperformed IVV only in 2017, 2018, and 2020.

Year Active Return
October–December 2015 -0.85%
2016 -3.42%
2017 0.73%
2018 0.43%
2019 -0.50%
2020 0.14%
2021 -1.60%
2022 -0.56%
2023 -1.23%
2024 -0.72%
January–September 2025 -1.64%

Calculated by the author using data from Portfolio Visualizer

But in my view, a much more unnerving issue comes to light when risk metrics are factored in.

The problem is that over the October 2015–September 2025 period, GSLC not only delivered a weaker annualized return but also had a much deeper maximum drawdown than IVV (September 2022). Besides, the risk-adjusted returns (the Sharpe and Sortino ratios) were below those of the S&P 500 ETF.

GSLC vs. Large Blend ETFs

While not having an edge over IVV, GSLC has also not achieved anything particularly special in the Large Blend ETF subclass it represents. For instance, it has delivered below-median total returns for the last five and ten years.

GSLC Portfolio Analysis

As of October 14, there were 430 stocks in the GSLC portfolio, with the key ten accounting for 34.6% of the net assets and NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA) being the top name.

It is true that the GSLC portfolio is leaner than that of the S&P 500 ETF (503 equities). Still, they hold nearly the exact same companies, as illustrated by the overlap.

Parameter GSLC IVV
Stocks present in both GSLC and IVV portfolios 94.63% 93.74%

Calculated by the author using data from Seeking Alpha and the ETFs

A few examples of companies that have qualified for the S&P 500 but not for the GSLC portfolio that I should mention are as follows:

Company Weight in IVV
The Goldman Sachs Group, Inc. (GS) 0.41%
Union Pacific Corporation (UNP) 0.24%
Prologis, Inc. (PLD) 0.19%

Data from IVV

Looking at the key ten companies in GSLC and IVV, we see that a few differences can still be spotted. For example, the Goldman Sachs ETF is just a little underweight in NVDA, and this is in part the reason why its growth characteristics are weaker than IVV's (I will be giving more details on that shortly).

Company Weight in GSLC, as of October 14 Weight in IVV, as of October 14 Sector Market Cap, $ billion
NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA) 7.50% 7.73% Information Technology 4,374.73
Microsoft Corporation (MSFT) 6.33% 6.72% Information Technology 3,817.45
Apple Inc. (AAPL) 6.27% 6.47% Information Technology 3,677.00
Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN) 3.41% 3.70% Consumer Discretionary 2,307.78
Alphabet Inc. (GOOGL) (GOOG) 3.15% 4.53% Communication 2,972.49
Meta Platforms, Inc. (META) 2.83% 2.71% Communication 1,780.23
Broadcom Inc. (AVGO) 2.28% 2.85% Information Technology 1,625.11
JPMorgan Chase & Co. (JPM) 1.29% 1.46% Financials 822.32
Tesla, Inc. (TSLA) 1.21% 2.12% Consumer Discretionary 1,427.29
Berkshire Hathaway Inc. (BRK.B) 1.11% 1.60% Financials 1,069.75

Created by the author using data from the ETFs and Seeking Alpha. Weights of GOOG and GOOGL were summed up by the author

GSLC vs. IVV: Sector Mix

While GSLC is offering nearly the same sector mix as IVV, their exposures are not totally identical.

The most notable differences are as follows:

  • GSLC is overweight in consumer staples, financials, and consumer discretionary.
  • It is underweight in energy, materials, and real estate.

Factor Exposures: GSLC Portfolio Has a Slight Value Tilt, Weaker Growth and Momentum Characteristics

So what factor story should investors expect here? In short, nearly the same as IVV's, though with a few subtleties: valuation is a little more comfortable, growth is weaker, and exposure to the momentum factor is smaller, while quality is mostly better. Let us address all the factors one by one.

Size And Value

GSLC and IVV have similarly large exposures to the trillion-dollar league (34.09% and 38.43%, respectively), but IVV is still heavier in these names, so its weighted average market cap is 11% higher.

Overall, the GSLC portfolio appears slightly better valued, with a smaller adjusted Price/Sales ratio (I removed triple-digit outliers for both portfolios), a marginally more comfortable adjusted WA earnings yield, and a larger allocation to stocks with a B- Quant Valuation grade or higher.

Growth

One of the basic concepts in equity research is that better value characteristics have a strong correlation with weaker growth. In other words, the higher the earnings yield, the lower the EPS growth rate. What we see here is that this is exactly the case with GSLC, which is illustrated by the forward revenue, EPS, and EBITDA growth rates, as well as its allocation to companies with a B- Quant Growth grade or higher.

Quality

The quality factor is where GSLC is doing a little bit better than the S&P 500 ETF, as it is offering higher weighted average Return on Assets and Return on Total Capital, plus it is sporting a larger allocation to stocks with a B- Quant Profitability grade or higher.

Momentum And Low Volatility

GSLC's exposure to stocks with remarkable momentum characteristics (a B- Quant Momentum grade or higher) is smaller than IVV's (49.67% vs. 50.73%, respectively).

At the same time, I would not say that there is any notable difference in their exposures to the low volatility factor, as illustrated by the WA 24-month and 60-month beta coefficients.

Final Thoughts

To sum up, while having a rather interesting strategy, GSLC has delivered results that cannot be called convincing or desirable. Alas, I see neither adequate upside capture nor reliable downside protection, which means this ETF is a questionable choice regardless of the market trajectory.

So at the end of the day, while I love sophisticated multifactor strategies, considering IVV has an even lower expense ratio (3 bps vs. 9 bps) and a much more appealing track record, I see little reason to defend a bullish view on GSLC.

data.title: Envista: Consider Survey Findings And Peer Disclosures (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
I've turned "Neutral" on Envista Holdings Corporation (NYSE:NVST). The read-across from sector polls and its listed comparable is mixed.

My earlier January 22, 2025, update rated NVST as a "Buy," considering its profitability upside.

Industry Surveys Point To Solid Growth And Higher Margins In Q3

NVST's upcoming results will be disclosed on Oct. 30.

Its 2Q25 normalized turnover was +6% higher on a year-on-year basis.

I'm projecting a superior high-single-digit (8%) topline expansion in the Jul-Sep period this year.

"American Dental Association's" or "ADA's" recent quarterly poll of dentists has positive read-throughs for NVST. The proportion of survey participants who are "somewhat or very confident" in their own "dental practice" rose from 2Q2025's 50% to 3Q25's 54%.

Also, 22% of U.S. dental professionals who were involved in the ADA study have "made major equipment purchases" in the third quarter of '25. This is equivalent to a +2ppts (percentage points) QoQ improvement.

Separately, UBS Group (UBS) published a report (inaccessible in public) titled "U.S. Orthodontia Survey" on Oct 16. This publication detailed "survey responses" from dental professionals regarding "their usage of clear aligner and traditional wire & bracket treatments specific to 3Q25 activity." The bank estimates that Envista should have achieved a +7% "aligner share gain" among orthodontists on sequential terms in the latest quarter.

It's reasonable for me to assume that NVST's Q3 sales increase should be better than that for the preceding quarter. My forecasts also suggest that the firm's EBITDA-to-revenue will widen by 200bps (basis points) from 12.4% for 2Q25 to 14.4% in 3Q2025.

ADA's questionnaire highlighted that two-thirds of American dentists have "increased their fees" year-to-date. The mean rate hike was +6.7%. I think there's leeway for NVST to adjust its own pricing upwards, in line with what customers have done.

The group referred to "automation of our factory, improving design cycle time, reducing our G&A costs" as the main initiatives to push up margins at the Q2 briefing. This explains why it is confident of guiding for a 2.2ppts rise in EBITDA-to-sales to 14.0% for full-year FY25.

I'm optimistic about NVST's 3Q25 showing, and expect investors to react favorably to its earnings release at month-end.

Chinese Operations Could Face Further Challenges Judging By Peer's Comments

NVST revealed in its 10-K that "we generate approximately 9% of our annual sales from Greater China." In the filing, it also noted that "the U.S. and the Greater China region represent key growth drivers for this (dental products) industry."

You would be right to view the "People's Republic of China" or "PRC" as a critical market for Envista. But NVST's China unit has fared poorly in recent times, and things could get even worse going forward.

The company shared at the 2Q25 analyst call that its Chinese division's 1H25 revenues were "down 20% to 30%" YoY due to the nation's "volume-based procurement" or "VBP" policy.

Envista's 10-K describes VBP as a "series of centralized reforms instituted in China on both a national and regional basis that has resulted in significant price cuts for medical and dental consumable products."

Straumann (OTCPK:SAUHF) (OTCPK:SAUHY), NVST's peer, met with the sell-side in late-September, before entering the black-out period for Oct 29's 3Q25 results release.

One of the brokers, Bernstein, issued a research note (not publicly available) dated Sep 29 with the title "Straumann: Key Themes" outlining their conversations with SAUHF's management. SAUHY mentioned that "China is already seeing some signs of VBP 2.0 (2nd round) negatively impacting patient flow." It anticipates that the new phase of "volume-based procurement" could possibly start in 1Q26.

Envista's revenue derived from the PRC is likely to fall in 4Q25. Local consumers are likely to defer their dental procedures in the near term, given that pricing would be on a downtrend post-VBP2.0. This is consistent with SAUHY's observations cited above.

Going into 1H26, a recovery in NVST's Chinese volumes will come at the expense of weaker "average selling prices" or "ASPs" and margins when VBP2.0 starts. As a reference, VBP1.0 saw "dental implant prices drop 55%" two years ago, according to China's media outlet "Yicai Global."

I fear that NVST's financials in the coming quarters will take a hit from this development.

The Final Word

My rating for NVST is lowered from a "Buy" to a "Hold." I see Envista doing well in 3Q2025, taking into account the insights from surveys conducted by industry analysts and analysts.

But this outperformance might not be sustainable, given potential China-related headwinds for the final quarter of '25 and early-2026.

NVST trades at a "price-to-earnings growth" or "PEG" of 0.93x (16.7/17.9), or close to 1, indicative of fair valuation. This is calculated based on its next twelve months' normalized P/E of 16.7x and the consensus FY24-28 EPS CAGR of +17.9%. These metrics were sourced from S&P Capital IQ.

data.title: TSMC: Buy The Backbone Of The Semiconductor Industry
data.description:
Introduction

With Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Limited aka TSMC (NYSE: NYSE:TSM) just releasing its Q3 earnings, I believe that now is the right time to evaluate one of the most critical companies in the semiconductor industry and maybe the world. To put things in perspective, the company currently has more than 70% market share in the foundry business, which has tremendously gained from the transition towards fabless designers.

With this critical aspect in my mind and the semiconductor company appreciating more than 51.8% YTD, I want to see if TSMC could still be a great opportunity following its earnings and windfalls from the current age of AI.

Operation Overview

TSMC’s core is the pure-play foundry model, which solely focuses on manufacturing integrated circuits for other companies based on their designs. This approach directly allows fabless companies to bring their R&D and innovations to life thanks to the close partnership with TSMC. And that not only helps TSMC, but also makes companies such as Nvidia and AMD proliferate and dominate their product segments. TSMC is also gaining from its pursuit of advanced manufacturing process technologies, which consist of continuously shrinking the size of transistors and improving energy efficiency. It was the first to commercialize 7-nanometer and 5-nanometer process technologies and is at the forefront of 3-nanometer.

With all of that in mind, TSMC is able to produce wafers for many different applications such as smartphones, Internet of Things ("IoT"), Automotive, and high-performance computing. The wafer side of the business has generated $76.5B in revenues in 2024.

TSMC also includes other product revenue, consisting of advanced packaging and testing services, which have become critical factors as the complexity of semiconductors increases. Many packaging solutions include CoWos and InFO, becoming vital to integrate multiple chips into a single device. The segment also includes Mask Making, which consists of photomasks that are high-precision quartz plates containing a microscopic blueprint of integrated circuits. This segment generated $11.56B for the LTM.

Current Dynamics and Q3 earnings

First, Taiwan Semiconductor announced a strong beat on both its top and bottom lines with revenue coming in at $33.1B, a beat of over $1.54B and most importantly, a 40.8% Y/Y growth. Q3 GAAP EPADR came in at $2.92, which was a beat of around 32 cents as well. There have been great trends announced with shipments of 3-nanometer accounting for 23% of overall wafer shipments. And from that, High-Performance Computing or HPC remains the largest revenue contributor at 57% of total sales.

Looking ahead, the company updated its forecast and raised its 2025 revenue growth to the mid-30% range following the quarterly beat. Furthermore, TSMC is expecting revenue to come in between $32.2B and $33.4B for the fourth quarter, while the initial consensus estimate came in at $31.35B. The company stated that it continues to see strong demand for the leading-edge process technologies and robust AI demand that pushed capital spending to $42B for the year.

In all, I believe that this was again a great quarter for the Taiwanese-based semiconductor company, with the strongest double beat since 2021, and while the company’s stock has greatly appreciated, this leaves greater room for growth ahead.

Outside of earnings, the growth of AI and HPC will continue to garner sustained demand that could last for years and years. Demand coming in from autonomous driving would accentuate the company’s push into energy-efficient chips. Furthermore, the current race towards more and more complex chipsets is highly beneficial for TSMC, as it has poured billions into staying ahead of the curve, which now allows the company to flex its dominance on the market with premium pricing, as it is the only company on earth enabled to produce what it produces.

Valuation

Taiwan Semiconductor is certainly not the cheapest company available on the market, though it has a relatively fair P/E GAAP of 25.41, lower than peers such as Tower Semiconductor. It also has a respectable EV/EBITDA of 14.91, far lower than Intel (INTC). TSMC is also a great FCF generator with over $30.6B FCF produced in the last twelve months, though the relentless rise in share price only translates into a FCF yield of 3.1%.

Outside of sheer metrics, there is also intangible value behind TSMC, such as very close relationships with industry titans like Nvidia that continuously justify the capital spending that TSMC does in terms of technical advancements. They share each other's detailed roadmaps, reducing the chance of misallocated capital. Furthermore, TSMC is the only leading-edge foundry that does not own its own branded products, giving customers comfort in the lack of conflict of interest and equal access to TSMC’s offering.

TSMC has posted stellar growth over the past 3 years, thanks to the rise in demand in chipsets, mainly riding the AI boom, with revenue and net income both growing at more than 21% CAGR, well above any strict foundries. Another key development that helped TSMC’s growth and should continue in the future would be that the technological gap with peers, mainly smaller ones, that remains extreme. Both TSMC and Samsung (OTCPK:SSNLF) are the only foundries in the world capable of producing 5 and 3-nanometer chips at scale, but Samsung has seen poor yields at 3nm, thus reducing its market share in a largely dominated market by TSMC.

There is a chance that Intel (INTC) could come as a competitor with its 14A node and mainly as the U.S. government and other partners are strongly behind Intel to rise back from the proverbial ashes, but I do not see how that would derail TSMC and its growth, and I believe it will come mostly at the detriment of Samsung.

TSMC is also a juggernaut in terms of profitability with its gross margin reaching 58.58%, vastly up since 2019 at 46.4%, while net income margin is now standing at a staggering 42.5%. This is a result of TSMC enjoying some of the best structural cost advantages on the market, mainly compared to Intel. Both Intel and Samsung have to firewall client-owned designs so as not to collude for their own designs, which add direct costs to the operation.

Furthermore, the sheer scale of TSMC with its massive foundries has been able to offer economies of scale, and this should continue as TSMC apparently has more than 90% market share in AI-related computing chips. This should continue the company’s efforts to improve its margins as well as capital efficiency metrics. Currently, TSMC has a great ROE at 34.3 and a ROIC of 31.1%.

Risks

First of all, TSMC is headquartered and has most of its manufacturing facilities in Taiwan, which has been a persistent ground for political tension with China. This has created threats for the country, mainly military conflicts or blockades, especially in recent years. If it were to be the case, this could be catastrophic for TSMC’s production and create severe shortage for all companies involved in the space. TSMC also has the ability to kill its most sophisticated chipmaking machines in the event of a Chinese invasion, which drastically reassures the U.S.

Furthermore, TSMC has taken steps to diversify its manufacturing capabilities by building in the U.S., as highlighted by its $100B plan to build five new factories in the country, creating a safe geopolitical manufacturing hub. I see it as a key positive for the company as it gives insurance in the case of an invasion.

The semiconductor industry is also highly cyclical, which is prone to periods of booms and busts, and though the AI demand is currently highly strong, it is prone to downturns reducing demand for the company’s products and hurting sales.

Conclusion

TSMC has the dominant position in wafer production, that’s for sure, especially as Samsung is facing yield issues and Intel is still years away from being a viable producer for fabless semiconductors. It is enjoying a great dominant position at a time when companies are more and more reliant on its manufacturing and innovation prowess. I rate the company as a Buy.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: UnitedHealth: Bargaining Power And Tariff-Driven Generics Could Improve Margins
data.description:
Thesis

UnitedHealth Group Incorporated (NYSE:UNH) has been a tricky one to value in recent times. On one hand, we're seeing a lot of increasing pressure on the company, be it through lawsuits or dealing with a spike in medical usage. However, for many, the stock was just too low when it was back in the mid-$200 range for shares to not see some recovery. Now, making its slow recovery up to the mid-$300 range, investors are starting to question whether UNH can show better margins on 3Q25 earnings at the end of the month. This would, of course, mean showing us a better Medical Loss Ratio (MLR). Now, I see a couple of tailwinds for improving that ratio figure, and we may see a much more bullish outlook for management sooner rather than later. Firstly, as we all know, the reimbursement rates for Medicare services are showing a small upward trend heading into 2026. The other area I see playing a factor is tariffs. Trump, of course, wants U.S.-based alternatives to be favored, and UNH can utilize this push to its advantage. I see them favoring domestic generic brands in the short term to pressure big pharma into renegotiating their rebates. For UNH, getting better rebates on certain big-name drugs should help push that MLR value a bit more in their favor.

Last Earnings Review

UNH reported 2Q25 earnings back in late July. As a quick recap, adjusted earnings were at $4.08 per share, down from the $6.80 we saw in the year-ago quarter. Back then, they clearly struggled with elevated medical costs and higher utilization across both Medicare Advantage and commercial lines. Consolidated revenues were up 13% year over year to a hefty $111.6 billion. This figure was mainly driven by growth in UnitedHealthcare and Optum, particularly Optum Rx and Optum Insight. Management noted $1.2 billion of discrete headwinds, which included more losses to come in the individual exchange business, which weighed somewhat on results. Despite all this pressure, they still reaffirmed their full-year adjusted EPS outlook, which was to see at least $16.00 by the end of 2025. Management also highlighted ongoing operational initiatives aimed at stabilizing margins. They also placed a heavy focus moving forward on controlling the Medical Loss Ratio.

A Balance Between Utilization And CMS

We’ve seen that one of the clearest near-term aids for UNH’s medical cost ratio has been that steady increase in Medicare Advantage. So essentially, reimbursement rates from the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS). What I mean by this is that those higher Medicare Advantage payment rates come from CMS, so the federal agency that oversees the Medicare program has decided to increase the reimbursement amounts it pays insurers like UNH for each Medicare Advantage member they cover. This figure is what we want to see go up; however, the pathway for these reimbursement payments to increase aren’t so simple. CMS will look at expected healthcare costs across the whole system, and then it sets a benchmark rate. So the per-member-per-month amount that insurers receive to provide coverage. When we see CMS raise that rate, like the 3.70% increase for 2025, and the 5.06% bump for 2026, it directly helps out UNH’s top line for its Medicare Advantage business. Now, these payments are crucial to UNH; they’re basically there to help cover the cost of care. So a higher reimbursement helps offset this rising medical utilization we’re seeing. But keep in mind, it doesn’t eliminate the pressure for UNH entirely. I like to view it as an important tailwind that points in the direction of UNH maintaining stable profitability even when care costs become very elevated, like we’re seeing now. In the table below, we can see how that utilization growth peaked through 2023 and 2024, so that all the pressure that rising care volumes are putting on costs. However, the CMS payment increases are starting to move higher, starting off below utilization growth, but by 2026, they should actually surpass it. That’s the tailwind we’re talking about here; we need higher reimbursement, finally catching up to the growth in care usage. At that point, I’m expecting UNH’s margins to stabilize somewhat.

For this year, CMS finalized a 3.70% average payment increase for MA and Part D plans, the prescription drug coverage portion of Medicare. I thought this was a meaningful bump over 2024's 1.03% rise. Then, for 2026, CMS went even further to help out with those payments. They approved a 5.06% average boost year over year. That’s a bump for next year that has been the largest upward adjustment in several cycles that I’ve seen. It’s also well above market expectations heading into the rule’s release, in my view. So why do these payment percentages help so much? Well, the Medicare Advantage segment is the single biggest and also the most profitable segment for UNH. It covers roughly half of all Medicare beneficiaries across the U.S. This higher reimbursement is somewhat of a buffer zone, helping out against the elevated medical utilization trends heading into 2026. So anything that helps alleviate pressure on UNH's margins is a good thing for me. UNH needs as much room as possible to absorb higher claim volumes, especially for outpatient surgeries/cardiac procedures, which are those high-cost services that spiked post-pandemic. Now, the key here for UNH is to absorb those higher volumes, but without it cutting into their medical loss ratio; we don't want to see that spiral further upward. In practical terms, we can say every 1% improvement in CMS payment rates will translate directly into incremental revenue per member that offsets care cost inflation. The MLR essentially gives us an idea as to what percentage of every premium dollar goes into paying for medical aid versus what’s left for administrative costs and profit.

Part D Plan Management

Another lever that we need to see UNH control moving forward would be Part D plan management. This is, of course, done via the Optum Rx factor in controlling the MLR. Optum Rx serves roughly 1.67 billion adjusted scripts each year. It’s a significant scale that should allow UNH to negotiate better rebates. This would mean coming to better terms with pharmaceutical manufacturers for higher rebates on brand-name drugs. One way I see an access into renegotiating pharma rebates is by using the recent tariffs onset by President Trump. The president wants to essentially make domestic alternatives more attractive, especially the generics. I don’t see Optum Rx minding this push at all; they can steer patients toward the lower-cost domestic alternatives within the formulary. It would mean the insurer doesn’t have to pay the full cost of imported brands. So if those domestic alternatives become more cost-competitive, UNH has its bargaining power right there. The message should be clear: ''If your drug isn’t competitively priced or backed by sufficient rebates, we can just favor generics instead.'' If UNH uses this kind of leveraging tool among all this tariff uncertainty, the manufacturers are incentivized to offer those better rebates to help UNH with margins down the line. Now, when it comes to MLR, since those pharmacy costs are part of the total medical costs, UNH, by directing utilization toward lower-cost alternatives and then capturing larger rebates later, should reduce the overall spend per member. I see this as helping UNH keep the medical loss ratio in check, even if we see utilization or total claims rising going into 2026.

Valuation

UNH shares are trading around $361.15. By multiple metrics, its valuation looks pretty attractive compared to the rest of the health care sector. The forward price-to-sales ratio sits at 0.73x, and the forward EV-to-sales ratio at 0.86x. Both are well below the sector medians of 4.22x and 3.75x, respectively. It seems UNH, despite being on the slow recovery up from those summer lows, can still be picked up at a discount to peers. The company still has solid margins and cash flow; its price-to-cash flow on a forward basis is at 17.34x compared to a sector median of 14.45x. So on this basis, there’s still a moderate premium for quality earnings. Even on a forward price-to-earnings basis, UNH sees a 22.17x multiple vs. a sector median of 18.59x. But this slightly higher multiple seems justified given UNH’s position in Medicare Advantage.

Into The Next Earnings

Looking ahead, UNH’s next earnings are set to report in late October. We should then get more of an idea as to whether levers are playing out in practice. Investors should, of course, watch out for the medical loss ratio, particularly within Medicare Advantage and Part D. I’ll also be watching Optum Rx’s execution in capturing rebates; we may hear management touch upon this. Strong conviction in these areas would tell me the company is actually trying to manage cost pressures while maintaining revenue growth.

data.title: Huntington Bancshares: No Signs Of Credit Issues
data.description:
Today we continue our critical Q3 regional bank coverage with Huntington Bancshares Incorporated (NASDAQ:HBAN). We have moved to the sidelines on this name after a winning trade earlier this year. We are not seeing signs of a credit crunch or credit issues. There were some whispers of this yesterday and fears over bad loans brewing in other regionals. However we like that the bank organically is growing, and is growing by acquiring Veritex, expanding Huntington's presence in Texas for $1.9 billion. And, the approvals are in for the merger, and that should close on Monday (10/20/2025). Let us discuss the just-reported Q3 results.

Huntington Bancshares' Q3 earnings headline performance

On the headline results, Huntington Bancshares performed slightly with expectations. The bank reported Q3 adjusted earnings per share of $0.41, up a strong $0.07 from the sequential quarter and up $0.08 from a year ago.

Both loans and total deposits continue to grow reliably. We want to see growth in loans and deposits as well as see the bank maintain strong asset quality. Here in Q3, average total deposits increased $1.4 billion, or 1%, compared to the sequential Q2. These deposits were also up $8.3 billion, or 5%, from last year's Q3. We are also seeing loan balances continue to grow. Average total loans and leases increased $2.8 billion, up 3%, from the sequential quarter to $135.9 billion. Compared to a year ago, these loans were up $11.4 billion, or 9%. Huntington consistently has seen growth in these key metrics.

Margins are also strong. Here in Q3, net interest margin increased another 2 basis points to 3.13% from 3.11% last quarter, and is up 15 basis points from a year ago. Since we have been covering Huntington, the bank has enjoyed a higher net interest margin on average relative to peers. With the strong margins and net interest income, the company raised its 2025 guide for net interest income, which was positive. And we will say, despite some systemic concerns in the space, asset quality here remains stellar.

Huntington Bancshares asset quality strong

Asset quality metrics were still strong Q3. Credit looks good. Lending looks good. Net charge-offs were just 0.22% of average total loans and leases for the quarter, though this was a 2 basis point softening from 0.20% in Q2, but better than 0.26% in Q1. We like to see this low level of charge-offs especially while loans are going up. That said, the allowance for credit losses of $2.6 billion, held firm at 1.86%, of total loans and leases at quarter end, compared to Q2. And the efficiency of the bank has always been strong here. It did improve from 59.0% in Q2 to 57.4%. Solid improvement. Tangible book value increase $0.41 to $9.54, and this is up 10% from a year ago.

Final thoughts

Organic growth is strong here, and the 2025 outlook was upped slightly. We like that on Monday the acquisition of Veritex will be complete. Again that is going to help Huntington by expanding in Texas. The all-stock deal will add over 30 new branches to the portfolio, and bring in about $13 billion in new assets. We think that as shares pull back further, the buy case gets strong, but for now we continue to rate it a Hold.

data.title: Washington Trust Bancorp Is Showing Progress (Upgrade)
data.description:
I never enjoy turning bearish on a business. That's why I was unhappy when, in early July of this year, I made the difficult decision to downgrade Washington Trust Bancorp, Inc. (NASDAQ:WASH) from a "Hold" to a "Sell." This decision was based on continued weakness when it came to the firm's balance sheet. Deposits had been declining, and profitability had been mixed. Whenever I rate a company a "Sell," I'm not necessarily expecting the stock to fall. Rather, I'm expecting the company to underperform the broader market for the foreseeable future. Since then, we have seen just that, with the stock falling 1% at a time when the S&P 500 is up 8%.

Looking at the data available today, I do see that there has been a slight improvement in deposits. Asset quality is looking better, and the stock is a bit cheaper. Given this combination of factors, I think that upgrading it once again back to a "Hold" is not necessarily a bad idea. This is especially true in light of continued improvements on both the top and bottom lines.

Being flexible in your thinking is important when it comes to investing. We should always reflect new data as it comes in. It just so happens that, after the market closes on Oct. 20, management will be reporting financial results for the third quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. Leading up to that point, analysts believe that revenue will rise while profitability will decline. This is actually interesting, but given the company's recent track record, it would be a bit surprising to me. If that does transpire, it very well could alter my opinion. But for now, I remain confident in my decision.

Things Are Looking Up Mostly

Almost always, when I write about banks, I like to touch on the balance sheet first. And the best place to start there would be the deposits. After all, these are essentially the fuel for the company to grow. In the past, I had been bearish about the firm because of declining deposits. They fell from $5.35 billion in 2023 to $5.12 billion last year. And in the first quarter of this year, they dropped further to $5.04 billion. But by the second quarter of this year, they did inch up ever so slightly to $5.05 billion.

What's more, this occurred at a time when brokered deposits for the institution remained low, at only $1.8 million compared to the $297.5 million reported at the end of last year. In fact, if we look at deposits that don't include brokered deposits, we would actually get an increase from the end of last year to the second quarter of this year, from $4.82 billion to $5.04 billion. According to management, this occurred even though competition for deposits remains intense and might be tied to some of the investments that the company has been making into technology to improve its customer experience.

Another thing that I like about the company is the fact that its uninsured deposit exposure is quite low. Typically, I like uninsured deposits to be no more than 30% of total deposits. As of the end of the second quarter, the firm had $1.80 billion in uninsured deposits, amounting to 21% of the total. But if we don't strip out the fully collateralized preferred deposits from the equation, this is a bit higher at 26%. Still, it is lower than the maximum that I like to see. So that's great.

Loans at the end of the most recent quarter amounted to just a hair under $5.10 billion. This essentially matches what the company had at the end of last year and represents an increase over the $5.06 billion the company had in the first quarter. The greatest exposure that the company has here is to commercial real estate, with $2.18 billion of its total loan portfolio falling under this category. But not far behind, at $2.10 billion, would be the residential real estate that it has exposure to. By comparison, other categories like commercial and industrial loans and home equity loans are quite small.

Naturally, we should touch on other aspects of the balance sheet as well. For the second quarter of this year, the company had securities of $971.3 million. That is up from the $917.5 million reported for the first quarter, and it compares nicely to the $916.3 million it ended last year at. Cash and cash equivalents, meanwhile, have also been on the rise, growing from $113.9 million in 2024 to $167.7 million in the second quarter. But the downside to this is that debt has also increased, rising from $872.7 million in the first quarter of the year to $1.02 billion in the second quarter. It is worth noting, however, that debt is still below the $1.15 billion the company had at the end of last year.

When it comes to the income statement, we have seen some nice improvements as of late. Thanks to changes in the company's balance sheet, the firm enjoyed an increase in its net interest margin from 1.83% in the second quarter of 2024 to 2.36% at the same time this year. This helped to push net interest income up from $31.1 million to $36.6 million. And for the first half of this year, the increase in net interest margin from 1.84% to 2.32% helped push net interest income up from $62.1 million to $71.8 million.

Non-interest income has also been on the rise. It totaled $17.1 million in the second quarter of this year, compared to the $16.7 million last year. According to management, this was driven by strength across multiple areas, including wealth management revenue that grew from $9.7 million to $10.1 million, mortgage banking revenue that expanded from $2.8 million to $3 million, and loan-related derivative income that shot up by over $0.6 million. The picture would have been even better had it not been for a $1 million gain on the sale of bank-owned properties that the company booked in the second quarter of last year. And for the first half of this year, these same trends largely continued, pushing non-interest income up to $39.7 million compared to the $33.8 million reported for last year. As a result of these improvements, net income has also been growing, hitting $13.2 million in the most recent quarter compared to $10.8 million last year. And for the first half of this year, the $25.4 million that the company achieved exceeded the $21.8 million reported one year ago.

There have been some other improvements with the company that are noteworthy. Specifically, shares have gotten cheaper from a valuation standpoint. If we project out results based on what we have seen already for this year, the company is trading at a price-to-earnings multiple of 10.7. This is just above the 10 maximum that I typically like to see. And, as the chart above illustrates, the firm is around the middle of the group, with three of its five competitors that I stacked it up against trading cheaper than it is. In the chart below, I did the same type of comparative analysis using the price-to-book and price-to-tangible-book approaches. In this case, the picture is not as attractive. Although the company is trading around book value, it is more expensive than four of the five companies that I stacked it up against. And on a price-to-tangible-book basis, three of the companies are cheaper than it is.

One thing I will also say is that we have seen some improvements when it comes to asset quality. In the first chart below, you can see the firm’s return on assets. At 0.80%, the company is still below the 1% minimum I like to see. But it's up from the 0.73% when I last wrote about it and is above three of the five companies that I stacked it up against. Return on equity is more attractive, as shown in the chart below, with a reading of 10.14%. This is above the 10% minimum I'd like to see and is also higher than the 9.63% that the company had when I last wrote about it. It also is above four of the five competitors.

We should also touch briefly on certain credit quality metrics. Specifically, I'm talking about non-performing assets as a percentage of total assets. Although we have seen an increase from 0.34% at the end of last year to 0.39% today, this is still below the 0.52% that the FDIC has calculated for all state-chartered banks nationwide. On top of this, with non-performing loans to total loans of only 0.51% and the total past due loans to total loans amounting to just 0.27%, I wouldn't say that the picture is necessarily bad. However, it has shown some deterioration since the end of last year. So that trend should be watched as time goes on.

As I mentioned at the start of this article, management will soon be reporting financial results for the third quarter of the 2025 fiscal year. The current expectation is for the business to generate revenue of $56.4 million. That would be up from the $48.5 million reported last year. Earnings per share, meanwhile, have been forecasted to decline from $0.64 to $0.46, which would take net profits down from $11 million to $8.9 million. There was a slight decline from the third quarter of 2023 to the third quarter of 2024. But there was nothing in either of those years that stuck out to me as a reason why last year's results would have been artificially inflated. And given the company's recent track record, I would find underperformance to be unusual. Regardless, it is something that investors should keep an eye on.

Takeaway

Based on all the data currently available, I continue to believe that Washington Trust Bancorp is far from a great prospect. But I do think the picture has gotten a bit better. Asset quality has improved, and shares are cheaper than they were previously. We have seen stabilization in deposits, and broker deposits are practically nonexistent. The income statement is also growing nicely. Given this combination of factors, I think that upgrading it to a "Hold" is the right choice.

data.title: Ghosts Of 2008: How Concerning Are The Subprime Auto Loan Delinquencies?
data.description:
Introduction

Here we go again, the regional banking sector (KRE) is collapsing, something that we already experienced two and half years ago following the SVB (OTC:SIVBQ) bankruptcy.

Back then, the main issue was related to a huge number of unrealized losses on long-term Treasuries, this time concerns a deterioration of the auto loan industry. In both cases, the underlying problem stemmed from a high-interest rate environment, a factor that the economy was not used to anymore.

In hindsight, we state that interest rates remained higher for a longer period than expected, and this is causing some cracks within the financial system.

Two years ago the Fed prevented a crisis spillover by introducing the Bank Term Funding Program (BTFP), namely the possibility to borrow funds using as a collateral the Treasuries’ face value. This maneuver inhibited the growing panic spread by the huge unrealized losses, which took up a significant part of the banks’ book value.

What is happening today is different. Even though the unrealized losses are not faded from banks’ balance sheets, investors fear a rapid deterioration of loan delinquencies, especially from the auto industry.

What is it happening?

The banking sector has plunged over the last few days, but the first red flags were already visible in September when both Tricolor and First Brands filed for bankruptcies. Why it was important?

  • First Brands operated in the auto parts industry and its bankruptcy derived from creditors’ worrying about the debt sustainability of the company. First Brands on paper didn’t show liquidity problem, but just because if was used to borrow through shadow banking. Compared to issuing a bond or borrowing from a bank, this private debt was kept off-balance sheet and this makes it harder to assess the overall financial health. Approximately, the total liabilities should be in $10 billion-$50 billion range, while assets might be just $1 billion-$10 billion. That’s concerning, since up until recently, this overleveraged company was able to refinance its debt pretty easily.
  • Tricolor was a company involved in the subprime auto loans business, so it lends money to people with a poor credit rating. The reason for the bankruptcy was probably more serious than First Brands, as the company has been accused of auto loan fraud. Basically, it was used to double-pledging its assets (mainly auto loans) to multiple lenders in order to get access to more money than it could have. This fraudulent behavior negatively affected multiple banks (mainly Barclays, JPMorgan, Fifth Third Bank), forced to write-off their loans.

Fifth Third (FITB) anticipated a loss on Tricolor asset-backed loans of $170 million-$200 million, while JPMorgan (JPM) has been hit by a $170 million charge-off. Peanuts for JPMorgan, but CEO Jamie Dimon pointed out that we shouldn’t underestimate what is going on:

My antenna goes up when things like that happen. I shouldn't say this, but when you see one cockroach, there's probably more. Everyone should be forewarned on this one.

These statements don’t leave too much room for interpretation: Tricolor and First Brands might not be an isolate case.

These doubts have been fueled by the recent earnings release of two important regional banks, Zions Bancorporation (ZION) and Western Alliance Bancorporation (WAL). Both tumbled more than 10%, as the first one was hit by a $50 million charge-off related to commercial loans defaults, and the second one had similar issues.

In other words, it seems that this fraudulent practice of overstating the value of the collateral is more frequent than previously expected. The biggest risk is that other companies followed this approach to get their loans, thereby unveiling a potential crack in the financial system. But what led companies to misbehave?

The answer probably lies in the aftermath of the Great Financial Crisis (2008). That crisis was so severe that induced regulators to make substantial changes in the way banks lend money and manage their capital requirements. More tight criteria took up the lending process, but companies didn’t experience right away the consequences. After all, from 2008 to 2021 interest rates were close to 0% most of the times.

Only when the Fed started to raise rates, the financial landscape evolved. Companies couldn’t borrow at low rates anymore, and since banks still follow tight lending policies, they needed another option to borrow money: this is where private debt and fraudulent activities step in.

About these two factors, as of now, we don’t know very much about them. For obvious reasons, companies don’t release their fraudulent activities in their financial statements, and private debt is not easily visible due to lack of public disclosure. With some effort, analysts can make estimates about the private debt amount, but it is complex since it comprehends off-balance sheet items.

This is probably the aspect that worries me the most, as the private debt issue is not something that can be easily measured. What if a lot of companies are hiding these debts on purpose? This goes beyond my expertise and I can only closely watch how things will evolve.

However, there are some public data that can help us to better assess the overall situation. Both Tricolor and First Brands were companies involved in the auto industry, and the first one had major headwinds with subprime auto loans. So, I suppose this is where we can find more red flags.

The Auto Loan 60+ Delinquency Index hit all-time high for subprime loans, 6.43%. Predicting a recession based just on this data is no-sense in my opinion, but we can’t deny that something is going on. U.S. households are struggling to meet their monthly auto loan installments, that’s a fact. Why it is so?

There are multiple reasons, the first one I think relates to the excessive cost of buying a new/used car.

For the first time in U.S. history, the average selling price for a new car was over $50,000 in September, so households have no alternative other than bearing a significant monthly cost if they need one. And the same goes for used cars as well.

Furthermore, it is not just the price paid, but also the interest burden that is sinking the U.S. middle class. For a used car, the average interest rate was 11.87%: no wonder delinquencies are rising.

How worrying is all this?

When media talks about subprime loan delinquencies, the association with the Great Financial Crisis inevitably comes up. However, I believe it is wise to stay rational and avoid making such a comparison.

The auto loan industry might even be in a worst situation, but we don’t have yet signs of a wide spillover. The issue seems industry specific as you can deduct from this chart.

Delinquency rate on all loans is not even close to what the economy experienced during the Great Financial Crisis. With that said, I don’t want to overlook what it is happening, as I really believe that U.S. middle class is facing a though period. Plus, the private debt needs further attention over the next months since we don’t know the magnitude of this phenomenon.

Anyway, changing our investment strategy because of this news I don’t think it is worth it. Every time I see reasons that could trigger a recession, but they are most of the time just false positives.

Consider that Fed Chair Jerome Powell recently stated the end of the quantitative tightening, and with more interest rates cuts ahead, financially distressed companies could face lower debt pressure. On top of that, long-term Treasury bonds yields are heading downwards, suggesting a relief for banks’ unrealized losses. As long as inflation doesn’t spike up, the Fed has enough room to heal the financial system’ cracks.

To some extent, I see this correction as healthy for the long-term trend.

We know that the stock market can’t just go up endlessly, so it won’t surprise me at all if it drops 5-10% from all-time high. This news, combined with government shutdown and the tariff war with China, seem all potential reasons to deflate the current market valuation. However, I don’t currently see them as catalysts for something more than a healthy correction.

data.title: Mercedes-Benz Faces Declining Prospects As Chinese Auto Industry Expands Globally
data.description:
“Make the world more dependent on China. Make China less dependent on the world.” – Xi Jinping, Politburo, May 2020

This month China expanded restrictions on rare earth and permanent magnet exports, a move that has the potential to impede or slow development of vehicles worldwide, especially battery-electric vehicles (BEVs).

Suppliers to the automakers require the materials for electric motors and hybrid systems delivered to assembly plants. Mercedes-Benz AG (MGBYY) (MGBAF) is among the automakers scrambling to assure a consistent source of supply, in part by stockpiling as much it can to avoid a production shutdown.

“If you come into such a risk situation, then we’re in constant, constant dialogue with our suppliers,” Jörg Burzer, Mercedes’ head of production, told reporters June 4 at a plant in Rastatt, Germany. “Naturally, we discuss with them what the best tool is for risk management, so physical buffers play a role.”

China, with 107 car companies, has the capacity to assemble 50 million vehicles or roughly 55% of the world’s current demand, compared with the 10 million vehicle in the U.S. None of China’s automakers wish to go out of business, so many are exporting – everywhere, for the moment, but to the U.S., which erected a 100% tariff on Chinese BEVs during the Biden administration to keep them out.

Falling prices and profits

Other automakers are cutting prices in China to stay alive, putting pressure on profit margins of foreign and Chinese brands even as most lose share. In the third quarter, Mercedes unit sales declined 12% worldwide, on the heels of declines in China (27%) and the U.S. (17%).

In 2021, Mercedes announced an “electric only” strategy by 2030, anticipating heavy reliance on BEVs, supported by more than 40 billion Euros. Earlier this year, the company announced a pivot to a mixture of BEVs, gas-electric hybrids and gasoline powertrains in order to meet changing consumer demand. So far, however, Mercedes hasn’t announced any write-offs or charges in connection with the strategy change.

Later this year Mercedes plans to introduce a new CLA sedan in the U.S., a BEV, that will sell for around $50,000 – a lower price point than its previous BEV models. Mercedes told Automotive News its goal “is to attract buyers of the Audi E-tron, BMW i4 and Tesla Model 3.” The German automaker had been planning on a $7,500 incentive from the U.S., which expired on October 1.

Protectionism abroad, especially in China, and increasing competition from exported Chinese BEVs in Europe have eroded Mercedes’s bottom line. The automaker’s earnings have declined over the past two years and the prospect is not good for a reversal given the rise of Chinese exports.

Notes global shipping specialist Maskura Logistics:

The European market has become increasingly dependent on Chinese production. Chinese-made vehicles now represent over 20% of EU battery-electric vehicle sales, up from just 3% three years ago. In the first nine months of 2024 alone, Europe imported approximately 581,000 vehicles from China, with Chinese brands accounting for roughly 8% of the total EU EV market share.

Protectionism spreading

Although the European Union has imposed tariffs on Chinese imports, analysts have opined the barriers aren’t likely to stanch the flood of vehicle exports from Chinese brands such as BYD, Geely, Chery, Leapmotor, Nio, Changan, Great Wall Motor and Xpeng. Tesla and other Western automakers such as Renault Group’s Dacia and BMW also are exporting EVs to Europe from China.

In response to tariffs, some Chinese automakers are planning to manufacture on the continent. A top producer, BYD, plans to initiate production in Hungary in 2026. Geely Holding Group, a major exporter, owns stakes in European brands such as Volvo and Polestar, which also export from China.

A key asset that Mercedes-Benz has in its favor – and which defies simple measurement – is its brand equity, the result of 150 years in the automobile business and status as a top maker of luxury automobiles. Prestige counts: notwithstanding impressive new competition from China, many buyers will always prefer Mercedes’s aspirational three-pointed star. But luxury brands aren’t impervious to spunky upstarts, as General Motors Co.’s Cadillac brand learned to its chagrin in the U.S. when Toyota invented the Lexus brand.

BYD, which builds BEVs exclusively, has its eye on top tier automakers as well as the middle and lower market segments. As Car and Driver reported “BYD introduced the Yangwang brand in 2023, and yes, the name is bound to elicit a few snickers from those with a juvenile sense of humor.” The nameplates U8 and U9 models each feature more than 1,000 hp and a price north of $150,000 – characteristics that surely will make the engineers at Mercedes, not to mention Audi, BMW and Tesla, pay attention.

Premium strategy

A countermeasure that Mercedes is deploying is to increase focus on higher priced models – Maybach, S-Class and G-Class models -- which tend to be more profitable than smaller and less expensive models. As pointed out by Agar Capital, Mercedes-Benz continues to generate decent cash flow, enough to cover its generous dividend.

From a long-term investment perspective, I remain cautious regarding MGBYY and MGBAF. Mercedes-Benz, with its impressive history, brand equity and engineering prowess, must take steps to match Chinese efficiency and technical innovation. The automaker also must manage its political alliances on three continents to maintain as level a playing field as possible with regard to trade and tariffs. Given the primacy of automaking to European economic strength, it’s hard to imagine that Germany will allow its companies such as Mercedes to falter without taking drastic steps, if needed.

Yet shareholders shouldn’t rely on the “too big to fail” umbrella – an assumption that failed to protect equity holders of GM in 2009. The auto industry is changing quickly and pervasively, with much overcapacity and resultant weak pricing hanging over every producer. Mercedes rates a HOLD for the time being, unless and until it can deploy a growth strategy against the Chinese tidal wave that’s lapping at its shores.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Iron Mountain: King Of Storage And Dispositions Offers Robust Return Prospects
data.description:
I previously covered Iron Mountain Incorporated (NYSE:IRM) in May 2025, discussing how the REIT's well diversified capabilities as a physical and digital storage provider had paid off handsomely, as the legacy Global RIM business delivered a rich cash flow business as they also capitalize on the ongoing data center boom.

Combined with the raised FY2025 guidance, the cheaper valuations, and the materialization of a much needed floor during the recent meltdown, I had believed its investment thesis remained compelling then.

In this article, I shall discuss how IRM's Matterhorn Strategy: Accelerating Growth has worked out brilliantly, with it triggering the accelerated Growth Businesses segment at a 4Y CAGR of +20% and the projected 2026 data center revenues of $1B (5Y CAGR of +25%).

Combined with the excellent server decommissioning prospects arising from the accelerated server refresh cycle, the rich total operational return prospect of 15.18%, and the last dividend raise by +9.7%, the REIT offers a unique way of tapping into the multi-year cloud super cycle, resulting in my reiterated Buy rating.

IRM Boasts A Well-Diversified Storage Ecosystem & Prudent Financing Method During The Ongoing Cloud Boom

IRM 1Y Stock Price

Since that reiterated Buy rating after a moderate retracement to the prior support ranges of between the $82s and the $89s, my Buy Zone has materialized by mid August 2025 indeed, with IRM also rallying by +13.6% since then.

A similar development has been observed in data center focused REIT peers in varying degrees, attributed to the cloud segment's "tight demand supply environment" where the world's largest five hyperscalers by market share have already hinted at higher AI related spending trends in 2026.

A similar development has been observed in many smaller (yet extremely well funded) players as well, including neoclouds and even, bitcoin miners turned HPC providers, with it apparent that we remain in the early innings of the multi-year cloud super cycle.

With IRM set to report their earning performance in November 05, 2025, readers may want to compare their upcoming performance against:

  • the FQ3'25 revenue growth guidance of +12% YoY, adj EBITDA at +14% YoY, and AFFO per share at +13% YoY offered in the FQ2'25 earnings call, against
  • the twice raised FY2025 guidance at +11.5% YoY, +13.8% YoY, and +12% YoY, respectively.

When compared to the FY2024 outperformance at +12.2% YoY, +13.7% YoY, and +10.1% YoY, respectively, the storage REIT has indeed been extremely successful in its diversification across physical ecosystem supporting information storage and retrieval to a hybrid of physical/ digital media ecosystem.

These reasons are also why IRM's Matterhorn Strategy: Accelerating Growth has worked out brilliantly, since the aggressive investments in its digital businesses (including Data Center FQ2'25 revenue growth by +26% YoY and Asset Lifecycle Management [ALM] by +70% YoY) have triggered the accelerated Growth Businesses segment at a 4Y CAGR of +20%.

This is on top of the projected expansion of the Growth Businesses revenue share at 28% by 2025, up notably from the 15% reported in 2021, with the REIT's excellent ability to cross-sell its vertically integrated storage solutions to its growing customer base already triggering the similarly rich bottom-line growth profile over the past few years.

For example, IRM's aggressive data center investments have already bore fruit, with 2025 to deliver nearly +30% YoY revenue growth and a long-term capacity growth from 450MW to 1.3 GW, with it providing an excellent line of sight to 2026 data center revenues of $1B (expanding at a 5Y CAGR of +25%).

This is significantly aided by the higher renewal pricing by +13% YoY, with the tight supply environment triggering the REIT's improved pricing power and consequently, richer top/ bottom-line performance.

At the same time, IRM's ALM services have also gone beyond physical records to its data center offerings through the decommissioning, data erasure, processing and disposition, and recycling of the partner hyperscalers' server components across the memory/CPU/GPUs, with it already comprising 40% of its ALM revenues.

With an accelerated server refresh cycle of "1 to 2 years, or up to 3 years" and Nvidia (NVDA) (and other infrastructure providers) already launching new AI chips on a yearly basis, I believe that the accelerated replacement cycle is likely to drive the REIT's long-term growth prospects.

Combined with the lower churn rate and the higher utilization across its vertically integrated storage offerings, I believe that IRM presents a unique breed of investment class for those looking to tap into the multi-year cloud super cycle.

IRM Valuations

Despite the notable upgrade from the pre-2013 data center ambition levels of 9.5x and the sector median of 14.73x, I believe that IRM still looks very compelling at FWD Price/AFFO valuations of 20.02x by the time of writing, thanks to the much needed correction from the October 2024 peak of 26.91x.

When comparing it to its data center REIT peers, including:

  • Digital Realty Trust (DLR) at FWD Price/ AFFO valuations of 25.71x/ 5Y mean of 22.30x,
  • Equinix, Inc. (EQIX) at 20.91x/ 24.16x, and
  • American Tower Corporation (AMT) at 17.68x/ 22.23x, respectively,

it is apparent that IRM remains reasonably valued at current levels.

The Consensus Forward Estimates

This is especially since IRM continues to report a healthy debt leverage of 5.0x, compared to DLR at 5.1x, EQIX at 3.5x, and AMT at 5.1x, along with the minimal shareholder equity erosion at -0.2% YoY, compared to its peers at +5.4% YoY, +3% YoY, and +0.2% YoY, respectively.

The latter is important indeed, since it allows the physical/ data center REIT to offer an estimated richer FFO per share growth at +12.7% through FY2027, compared to DLR at +8.2%, EQIX at +14%, and AMT at 0% over the same time period.

These metrics are also why I believe that the IRM management has been executing brilliantly, with the prudent financing method across debt and (inherent lack of) dilutive capital raises already triggering the richer earnings per share growth and the relatively more attractive valuations.

IRM Remains A Great Buy After A Slight Pullback

IRM 5Y Stock Price

For now, IRM has been consistently charting higher lows since the April 2025 bottom, albeit unable to break out beyond the established 2025 resistance levels of the $105s, as we enter the upcoming Q3'25 earnings season.

Despite this, I believe that the recent underperformance has been a boon, since the stock continues to trade nearer to my fair value estimates of $101.80, based on the management's FY2025 AFFO per share guidance of $5.085 at the midpoint (+12% YoY) and the current FWD Price/AFFO valuations of 20.02x (near to its peer group mean of 21x).

Based on the consensus FY2027 FFO per share estimates of $4.50 (expanding at a 3Y CAGR of +12.7%) and the current FWD Price/FFO valuations of 29.97x (not too far from the peer group mean of 26.5x), there appears to be an excellent upside potential of +32.3% to my long-term price target of $134.80.

At the same time, IRM's last dividend raise of +9.7% to $0.785 per share has well balanced the lower forward dividend yield of 3.08% as well, despite the underperformance against the 4Y mean yields of 3.92% and the sector median of 4.93%.

These have contributed to the extremely rich total operational return prospect of 15.18%, based on the 3Y AFFO per share growth at +12.1% and the forward dividend yield of 3.08%, compared to DLR at 12.4%, EQIX at 9.84%, and AMT at 8.34%.

As a result, I am reiterating my Buy rating for the IRM stock, with a few caveats to be discussed below.

The Risks Entering Q3'25 Earning Season

IRM's Technical Indicators

For now, IRM's technical indicators already suggest an overbought momentum and RSI, with it uncertain if the elevated trading volumes may break out of the first resistance line to retest the prior 2024 heights.

Combined with the potential tariff risks embedded in the wider market's bottom-lines from Q3'25 onwards and the ongoing trade discussions, my reiterated Buy rating comes with the caveat that investors observe the development for a little longer and only add nearer to the 50/ 100/ 200 day moving averages of $96s/ $98s for an improved margin of safety.

Despite the fair valuations, the well balanced legacy/ data center capabilities, and the excellent return prospects, some patience may be more prudent, given the potential downside of -5.7% from current levels in the short-term.

data.title: WaterBridge Infrastructure: Visible Earnings Growth
data.description:
Summary

I am positive on WaterBridge Infrastructure (NYSE:WBI). WBI offers infrastructure-like stability and visible, high-quality growth, backed by long-term contracts with investment-grade producers. It also has access to underutilized pore space through LandBridge, which positions it to capture demand for water management in the Permian Basin. With major projects like Kraken, Speedway, and Cotton Draw coming online in 2029, I have a positive outlook on the EBITDA growth over the foreseeable future.

Company overview

WBI is a water midstream operator with the largest produced water infrastructure network in the US, with a footprint concentrated in the Delaware Basin and some exposure to the Eagle Ford and Arkoma basins. WBI provides end-to-end water management solutions, including gathering, transporting, recycling, and handling produced water, for oil and gas [O&G] E&P customers under long-term contracts. As of August 31, 2025, per the S-1 filings, the WBI pro forma network included ~2,500 miles of pipelines and 197 produced water handling facilities, and also two energy waste management sites for non-hazardous waste.

Moat

I believe WBI’s most durable competitive edge is its partnership with LandBridge (this is WBI’s affiliate). This relationship gives WBI preferential access to the Permian Basin, which is an underutilized pore space for underground water disposal. For easier understanding, you can think of this as like holding exclusive rights to the only available landfill in a city that has waste growing faster than what its existing wasteland can hold. In the context of WBI, as O&G producers move into deeper, higher-water zones, the volume of produced water grows (or what is known as an increase in the water-to-oil ratio), and that creates both logistical and regulatory bottlenecks. To give you a sense of how urgent the situation is, per management estimates in the S-1 filings, without new infrastructure, they expect the Delaware Basin to face the risk of limited water-handling capacity by 2029, which is just 4 years away.

Building water infrastructure itself is already not a simple task; compound that with the stricter permitting environment, especially in New Mexico, where approvals for new disposal wells have become slow and uncertain, and the entire situation becomes even worse. And this is why the access to LandBridge is so important. Not only because it gives WBI the extra capacity, but also because it is located in business-friendly Texas and low-pressure geological zones, which gives customers the ease of comfort knowing there is capacity reliability. Without this, E&P customers will be worried, as a lack of disposal logistics would mean a stoppage in drilling programs, which is costly.

All of this dynamic also means that WBI has strong pricing power. And we already have proof of this playing out in recent project economics, where new contracts are being signed at higher rates. I don’t foresee this dynamic going away anytime soon, as it is just very difficult to build another competing asset within a short timeframe. Moreover, attempting to do so would require not only massive capital investment but also aggregating land with the right surface rights and geological conditions.

Demand backed by contracts

Aside from WBI’s moat, I believe the key anchor for the equity story is its visible growth, one that is supported by project backlog. WBI now has three large-scale developments ongoing—Kraken, Speedway, and Cotton Draw—that are going to fully come online by 2027. These projects are backed by long-term take-or-pay agreements and meaningful minimum volume commitments [MVCs] with investment-grade counterparties such as BPX Energy and Devon Energy. Barring major economic recession or natural disasters, this contractual agreement basically “locks in” WBI’s growth.

The unit economics are also very compelling as well. Per the disclosures, these projects are being built at highly attractive, low-single-digit build multiples and contracted at rates meaningfully above WBI’s existing portfolio average. This links back to my point above about WBI’s pricing power, and as these high-margin assets come online between now and 2027, I expect them to meaningfully expand operating margins and accelerate earnings growth.

On the existing asset base, it is attractive too. The current contracts are also supported by long-term, fixed-fee agreements with an average remaining life of ~11 years. Most (~70%) of these contracts have a tenure of 15 years or more and include annual inflation-linked escalators. On top of that, when you consider that WBI customers include some of the most active producers in the Permian Basin, such as BPX Energy (a BP subsidiary), Chevron, Devon Energy, and Permian Resources. It gives a lot more confidence in earnings growth visibility.

For context, during 1H 2025, ~73% of WBI’s water-related revenue came from customers with credit ratings of BB- or higher. Given the long-duration nature of these contracts (and thereby cash flow for WBI), having low counterparty risk is paramount. Importantly, WBI is not overly reliant on any single customer. Its top five customers accounted for ~51% of water-related revenue in 1H 2025, with the largest contributing 18%.

Valuation

Between FY24 and FY26, I modeled the same EBITDA growth rate as FY24, 9%, which I think is reasonable given the existing asset base is already contracted, and there are inflation-linked mechanics to ensure pricing can go up. From FY27 onwards, EBITDA growth should see a step up as those new assets come online at higher pricing with better profitability. My idea here is that it should go above 9%, in the low-teens range (hence, I used 12%). Should this play out, I think WBI should trade easily at 14x forward EBITDA or more since peers like American States Water Co., American Water Works, York Water, and Global Water Resources all trade in the 13/14x forward EBITDA range with earnings growth of low teens.

Investment Risk

My primary concern here is execution risk. The growth story is great, but it is really tied to how well it can bring these large-scale projects online. Any significant construction delays, cost overruns, or a failure to ramp up volumes could impact returns. It would also weigh on the EBITDA growth outlook since these are the main drivers for growth acceleration.

Conclusion

WBI is an attractive long. It has a visible earnings growth profile with catalysts in place to drive growth acceleration in the coming years. Moreover, its access through LandBridge is a massive moat that should continue to give it pricing power in the foreseeable future. As large-scale projects come online, margin expansion and earnings visibility should improve meaningfully, and with all this in mind, I think WBI should trade at 14x forward EBITDA, at least in line with peers. All in all, I give a buy rating.
